---
title: "Day 7 — Exercise: A second table and a join"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    code-copy: true
    code-overflow: wrap
    highlight-style: github
---

## Goal

Add a second table — `assay`, holding growth-rate measurements — and link it to the `strain` table from [exercise 01](01-sqlite-cli.qmd) with a **foreign key**. Then combine the two with [`NATURAL JOIN`](https://www.sqlite.org/syntax/join-operator.html) and [`INNER JOIN`](https://www.sqlite.org/syntax/join-operator.html) so you can ask questions like *"what is the mean OD₆₀₀ at 24 h for each species, in LB?"*.

After this exercise the first-half SQL material is done — the rest of day 7 is the same database, but driven from Rust.

## Background

We have measurements of optical density at 600 nm (OD₆₀₀) at 24 h of growth for each strain in our collection, across a few different media. Some strains have been measured multiple times, in different media or on different days. One row per *measurement*:

| Column | Type | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| `assay_id` | INTEGER | primary key |
| `strain_id` | INTEGER | **foreign key** → `strain(strain_id)` |
| `medium` | TEXT | LB / M9-glucose / TSB / BHI |
| `od600_24h` | REAL | optical density reading |
| `date_measured` | TEXT | ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` |

24 rows ship in [`data/assays.csv`](ex-sqlite-strains/data/assays.csv). Each `assay.strain_id` points to a row in `strain.strain_id`. That is what makes the data *relational* — one strain can have many assays, but every assay belongs to exactly one strain.

## The exercise

Reset to a clean state, then re-open. The seed file rebuilds the schema and inserts both tables:

```bash
cd day7/ex-sqlite-strains
rm -f strains.db
sqlite3 strains.db < data/seed.sql
sqlite3 strains.db
```

At the `sqlite>` prompt:

```sql
.headers on
.mode column
.tables             -- should show: strain   assay
.schema assay       -- inspect the new table
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM assay;   -- expect 24
```

### Step 1 — explicit `INNER JOIN`

Combine each assay with its strain by matching `assay.strain_id` to `strain.strain_id`:

```sql
SELECT s.strain_name, s.species, a.medium, a.od600_24h
FROM strain AS s
INNER JOIN assay AS a ON a.strain_id = s.strain_id
LIMIT 5;
```

- `AS s` and `AS a` are short aliases.
- `ON a.strain_id = s.strain_id` is the join condition.
- `INNER JOIN` (or just `JOIN`) keeps only rows where the condition matches on both sides.

### Step 2 — the same thing as `NATURAL JOIN`

Both tables have a column literally called `strain_id` and **only** that column matches across the two schemas. So you can write:

```sql
SELECT strain_name, species, medium, od600_24h
FROM strain NATURAL JOIN assay
LIMIT 5;
```

[`NATURAL JOIN`](https://www.sqlite.org/syntax/join-operator.html) joins on every same-named column automatically. Output is identical to Step 1. Convenient when your schema is designed with that in mind; rarely seen in real production code, because adding a column later can silently change the join. Use it once today; reach for `JOIN … ON` from then on.

### Step 3 — filter then join

Find every LB measurement for *E. coli* strains:

```sql
SELECT s.strain_name, a.medium, a.od600_24h, a.date_measured
FROM strain AS s
JOIN assay  AS a ON a.strain_id = s.strain_id
WHERE s.species = 'Escherichia coli'
  AND a.medium  = 'LB'
ORDER BY a.date_measured, s.strain_name;
```

The `WHERE` clause can mention columns from either table — the join happens conceptually first.

### Step 4 — aggregate after the join

Mean OD per species, restricted to LB:

```sql
SELECT s.species,
       COUNT(*)             AS n_measurements,
       ROUND(AVG(a.od600_24h), 2) AS mean_od
FROM strain AS s
JOIN assay  AS a ON a.strain_id = s.strain_id
WHERE a.medium = 'LB'
GROUP BY s.species
ORDER BY mean_od DESC;
```

[`AVG`](https://www.sqlite.org/lang_aggfunc.html#avg) and [`COUNT`](https://www.sqlite.org/lang_aggfunc.html#count) are *aggregate functions* — they reduce a group of rows to a single value. The output is one row per species; the grouping is what `GROUP BY` does.

### Step 5 — your turn

Write SQL to answer each of these. Try before peeking.

**(a)** For each strain, how many assays have been recorded?

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Answer"}
```sql
SELECT s.strain_name, COUNT(a.assay_id) AS n
FROM strain AS s
JOIN assay  AS a ON a.strain_id = s.strain_id
GROUP BY s.strain_name
ORDER BY n DESC, s.strain_name;
```
:::

**(b)** Which strain has the highest single OD₆₀₀ measurement, and in what medium?

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Answer"}
```sql
SELECT s.strain_name, a.medium, a.od600_24h
FROM strain AS s
JOIN assay  AS a ON a.strain_id = s.strain_id
ORDER BY a.od600_24h DESC
LIMIT 1;
```

`PAO1` in `LB`, OD = 1.97.
:::

**(c)** Across all media, what is the mean OD₆₀₀ for each *(species, medium)* pair? (Two grouping columns, not one.)

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Answer"}
```sql
SELECT s.species, a.medium,
       COUNT(*)              AS n,
       ROUND(AVG(a.od600_24h), 2) AS mean_od
FROM strain AS s
JOIN assay  AS a ON a.strain_id = s.strain_id
GROUP BY s.species, a.medium
ORDER BY s.species, a.medium;
```
:::

### Step 6 — a strain with no assays

To see why `INNER JOIN` is sometimes the wrong tool, insert a strain that has zero measurements:

```sql
INSERT INTO strain (strain_name, species, isolation_source, year_isolated)
VALUES ('JM109', 'Escherichia coli', 'lab derivative', 1985);

SELECT s.strain_name, COUNT(a.assay_id) AS n
FROM strain AS s
JOIN assay  AS a ON a.strain_id = s.strain_id
GROUP BY s.strain_name;
```

`JM109` does **not** appear — `INNER JOIN` dropped it because there is no matching `assay` row.

Swap the join type:

```sql
SELECT s.strain_name, COUNT(a.assay_id) AS n
FROM strain AS s
LEFT JOIN assay AS a ON a.strain_id = s.strain_id
GROUP BY s.strain_name
ORDER BY n, s.strain_name;
```

[`LEFT JOIN`](https://www.sqlite.org/syntax/join-operator.html) keeps every row from the left table (`strain`), filling `NULL`s where there is no match on the right. Now `JM109` shows up with `n = 0`. *This is one of the most common SQL bugs in practice* — a missing strain disappears because someone reached for `INNER JOIN` by reflex.

## Hints

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint — what columns are even in `assay`?"}
`.schema assay` always shows you. If you forget the table name, `.schema` with no argument shows every table's `CREATE` statement.
:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint — 'ambiguous column name: strain_id'"}
You wrote `SELECT strain_id` in a query that joins two tables both having a `strain_id`. Either qualify the column (`s.strain_id`) or, in `NATURAL JOIN`, leave it unqualified — `NATURAL JOIN` merges the two into one. Mixing the two patterns is what triggers the error.
:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint — `GROUP BY` and `SELECT` rules"}
Every non-aggregate column in `SELECT` should also be in `GROUP BY`. SQLite is forgiving and will pick *some* value for an unlisted column, but PostgreSQL and most other databases reject it as a hard error. Get into the habit early.
:::

## Where this leads

- [**Exercise 03**](03-read-from-rust.qmd) — open `strains.db` from Rust and read the rows out.
- Joins on three or more tables (a *transitive* join across `strain` → `assay` → `medium_metadata`) work the same way: chain more `JOIN ... ON ...` clauses.
- `JOIN` operations are the part of SQL that pays for indexes. Once a table has tens of millions of rows, an [`INDEX`](https://www.sqlite.org/lang_createindex.html) on the join column turns a 10-second query into a 1-millisecond one. Out of scope today; worth Googling once you hit it in real work.
